body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: unknown; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code's TUI surfaces API errors (network failure, provider 5xx, timeout) as an error notice in the conversation pane and returns the prompt to the user. The agent process keeps running — only the in-flight turn aborts. The runtime does NOT emit a normal `stop_reason=end_turn` or `system/turn_duration` for the aborted turn; whether anything terminal lands in the transcript at all depends on the failure mode and the Claude Code version.

### What irrlicht observes

The failure modes split into three buckets and irrlicht handles them with mixed confidence:

**Bucket A — runtime writes an error message to the transcript.** Some failure modes (provider 5xx after partial stream) cause Claude Code to write a synthesized assistant text describing the failure plus a terminal stop_reason. In that case the JSONL transcript carries an `end_turn` (or refusal) and `IsAgentDone()` fires the normal way — `working → ready` via classifier rule 2b. irrlicht sees this correctly.

**Bucket B — pure network error, no transcript write.** If the in-flight POST to the Anthropic API fails before any response chunk lands, Claude Code may simply abandon the turn without writing anything terminal. The transcript stops growing but the agent process stays alive. irrlicht has no event to trigger `IsAgentDone()` — the session would sit in `working` indefinitely under the strict JSONL-event path. The spec accommodates this with 'state reaches ready within sweep interval after the agent gives up'.

The sweep that bails this out is the inactivity-driven side of the session detector: a working session with no fresh JSONL activity does NOT today have a hard 'idle → ready' timer in the classifier (rule set is purely event-driven, see state_classifier.go). The closest existing mechanism is the stale-tool timeout — which was DELETED in PR #106 (memory: 'PR-106-bundles-two-flicker-fixes-1-97') after issue #97 / #102. So there is no general-purpose 'aborted turn → ready' sweep currently. The session goes back to ready only when the next user prompt arrives and the parser sees the new user message (which itself does NOT clear working — rule 4 keeps it in working). Strictly speaking, Bucket B can leave irrlicht stuck in `working` until the next assistant terminal message lands.

**Bucket C — Claude Code's own retry / error UI fires a turn_done.** Recent Claude Code versions emit `system/turn_duration` even on abnormal turn end as part of the stop-hook integration; the parser maps this to `turn_done` (parser.go:157-163) and `IsAgentDone()` fires via the primary path. This appears to be the increasingly common case but I do not have direct fixture evidence in the regression set.

### Why 'partial'

Buckets A and C land at ready correctly. Bucket B is the honest gap: a pure mid-stream network failure with no transcript epilogue can leave irrlicht's session in `working` indefinitely. The spec asserts 'state reaches ready within sweep interval' but irrlicht's classifier has no such inactivity sweep for the working state. This is a known shape of issue — the deleted stale-tool timer used to mitigate it but was removed as a flicker source (#97).

### What the recipe needs

To realistically exercise this cell the driver must induce a network failure mid-turn — typically by sending a long prompt then severing network (e.g. `pfctl` block on api.anthropic.com or `nc` proxy that drops). That's intrusive and recipe-fragile. A cheaper proxy: kill the TLS handshake by toggling an `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` env that points at a closed port. Either way, the variant-3 path of the session-end recipe (SIGKILL mid-turn) is a related but distinct test — it kills the AGENT process, not the API call.
caveats: There is no inactivity-driven 'working → ready' sweep in the current classifier. The deleted stale-tool timer (PR #106, removed for being a flicker source on Bash) used to mitigate this; nothing replaced it. Bucket-B failures can leave the session stuck in working until the next terminal event. This is the strongest argument for irrlicht_observes=partial rather than yes.

Distinguishing 'turn aborted by error' from 'user pressed ESC' (user-esc-interrupt scenario) is non-trivial — both can produce a quiet transcript tail. ESC has the `[Request interrupted by user]` text marker (recordUserInterruptFlags, parser.go:231); an API error has no such marker. If a future fix introduces an inactivity sweep, it must not race with ESC's explicit signal.

Newer Claude Code versions appear to emit `system/turn_duration` on abnormal exits too (Bucket C), shrinking the gap. Without a fixture in the regression set I cannot bound this precisely — confidence reflects that uncertainty.

Recipe construction is hard: inducing a real API failure requires manipulating network/env in a reproducible way. The recipe author should pick a method that the driver can toggle deterministically (e.g. point `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` at a port that refuses connections).

Capture status (as of 2026-05-22, issue #405): Bucket A is now captured via tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-5xx/main.go + tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-turn-aborted-by-error.sh. The mock sends message_start + a partial content_block_delta then emits SSE `event: error` (overloaded_error); claudecode appends a synthesized 'API Error: API returned an empty or malformed response (HTTP 200)' message with a terminal `stop_reason` and the session returns to ready cleanly. The committed events.jsonl shows the working→ready arc and the 4-phase expected.jsonl passes. Bucket B (silent network drop) and Bucket C (system/turn_duration on abnormal exit) remain documented gaps — confidence bumped 0.55→0.7 because Bucket A is now empirical rather than speculative.

sources.note: The four classification rules (PermissionPending, NeedsUserAttention, IsAgentDone, ESC/denial, default) are all event-triggered. No inactivity timer drives working→ready, so Bucket-B (transcript-silent) failures have no recovery path here.

Lines 157-163: `system/turn_duration` and `system/stop_hook_summary` both map to `turn_done`, which Bucket-C abnormal exits use as the recovery signal. Lines 282-303: the terminal stop_reason allow-list includes `refusal`, which covers some provider-error variants.

PR #106 removed the 15s `defaultStaleToolTimeout` because it was a flicker source on long Bash runs. That removal also took away the only inactivity-based safety net that could have rescued Bucket-B failures.
Lines 440-468 (`IsAgentDone`): both primary (turn_done) and fallback (terminal assistant) paths require a transcript event — neither fires on pure silence.

Anthropic-compatible mock used by the Bucket A recorder: streams message_start + partial content_block_delta then emits SSE `event: error` overloaded_error. Sibling of tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-429/main.go (used by token-quota-exhausted).

Bespoke recorder that launches claude --bare against the mock with ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL pointing at it. Sibling of tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-token-quota-exhausted.sh; uses the same --bare workaround so claude reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL from env (OAuth/keychain bypassed).
